coredata.dc:title: Enhanced H2 evolution and the interfacial electron transfer mechanism of titanate nanotube sensitized with CdS quantum dots and graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Jiayan
Long, Lizhen
Zhang, Lin
Luo, Hongtian
Yang, Li
Liu, Fuchi
Lv, Fengzhen
Kong, Wenjie
Liu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Synergistic the modulation of photon absorption capability and interfacial charge transfer of the photocatalyst are highly required for developing high-performance heterojunction photocatalysts. The ternary CdS-graphene quantum dots-titanate nanotubes (CdS-GQDs-TNTs) nanocomposite have been prepared by an in situ growth method. The physicochemical characterization reveals that the GQDs are firmly decorated on both inner and outer surface of TNT through the formation of Ti–O–C chemical bonding, and CdS QDs are loaded on the outer surface of TNTs through strong interfacial interaction. The intimate integrated CdS-GQDs-TNTs nanocomposite exhibits much superior photocatalytic performance toward H2 production compared with binary GQDs-TNTs and pure TNTs photocatalyst, which can be attributed to the combined interaction of the stronger visible light harvesting, the longer lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs, faster interfacial charge transfer rate, fast and long-distance electron transport pass. The interfacial charge transfer mechanism of CdS-GQDs-TNTs ternary composite are proposed based on photoelectrochemical measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanate nanotubes
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)
Hydrogen evolution
Charges transfer mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (Grant No. 2018GXNSFBA138007, 2018GXNSFBA281073, 2018GXNSFAA294021), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.11665007, 11664003), the State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University, CMEMR2018-B11), and the PhD Start-up Fund of Guangxi Normal University (Grant No. 2017BQ023, 2017BQ006), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. XYCSZ2019058).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (Grant No. 2018GXNSFBA138007 , 2018GXNSFBA281073 , 2018GXNSFAA294021 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11665007 , 11664003 ), the State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University, CMEMR2018-B11 ), and the PhD Start-up Fund of Guangxi Normal University (Grant No. 2017BQ023 , 2017BQ006 ), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. XYCSZ2019058 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs (a), and the Raman spectra of GQDs-TNTs (b).
TEM images of TNTs (a), GQDs-TNTs (c), CdS-GQDs-TNTs (e), and HRTEM images of TNTs (b), GQDs-TNTs (d), CdS-GQDs-TNTs (f).
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs, and (b) the corresponding pore-size distribution curves.
The high resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p (a), O 1s (b), C 1s (c), and Cd 3d (d) for the CdS-GQDs-TNTs composite.
UV–Vis DRS spectra of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs (a), and the corresponding Kubelka–Munk function spectra (b).

Photoluminescence spectra of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs (a). Transient photocurrent curves of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs under simulated sunlight irradiation (b), and under visible-light irradiation (c). EIS Nyquist plots of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs (d).
The courses of H2 evolution under simulated sunlight (a), and visible-light irradiation (b).
Mott–Schottky plots of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and pure CdS.
Diagrams of successively in-situ growth of GQDs and CdS on TNTs.
The charges transfer mechanism of the CdS-GQDs-TNTs ternary photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Enhanced H2 evolution and the interfacial electron transfer mechanism of titanate nanotube sensitized with CdS quantum dots and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics Science and Technology & Guangxi Key Laboratory of Nuclear Physics and Technology, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics Science and Technology & Guangxi Key Laboratory of Nuclear Physics and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Guilin 541004, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Guilin 541004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synergistic the modulation of photon absorption capability and interfacial charge transfer of the photocatalyst are highly required for developing high-performance heterojunction photocatalysts. The ternary CdS-graphene quantum dots-titanate nanotubes (CdS-GQDs-TNTs) nanocomposite have been prepared by an in situ growth method. The physicochemical characterization reveals that the GQDs are firmly decorated on both inner and outer surface of TNT through the formation of Ti–O–C chemical bonding, and CdS QDs are loaded on the outer surface of TNTs through strong interfacial interaction. The intimate integrated CdS-GQDs-TNTs nanocomposite exhibits much superior photocatalytic performance toward H2 production compared with binary GQDs-TNTs and pure TNTs photocatalyst, which can be attributed to the combined interaction of the stronger visible light harvesting, the longer lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs, faster interfacial charge transfer rate, fast and long-distance electron transport pass. The interfacial charge transfer mechanism of CdS-GQDs-TNTs ternary composite are proposed based on photoelectrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: The population explosion and industrial development has tremendously aggravated the environmental pollution and energy crisis. This situation creates a demand for clean and sustainable alternative energy source. Semiconductor photocatalytic water splitting is one of the most attractive methods to convert solar energy into clean and renewable hydrogen fuel. Since the pioneering work launched by Fujishima and Honda in 1972 [1], TiO2 has garnered enormous attention in photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis application due to its strong redox ability, long-term stability, low cost, and non-toxicity. However, the wide band-gap energy (3.0–3.2 eV) and fast recombination of photo-generated charges limit their further applications [2]. In the past four decades, considerable effort has been made to solve the above-mentioned issues. Coupling TiO2 with a narrow band-gap semiconductor to form heterojunction is proved to be a very efficient way to extend the visible light response of TiO2-based composite materials and promote the separation of photo-generated charge carriers, consequently improving the photocatalytic performance [3].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted tremendous attention in the field of energy conversion and environmental remediation for the sake of their distinct merits [4–8], including high-conductivity, environment friendly, excellent solubility, chemical inertness. Especially, the band-gap of GQDs are tunable in a wide energy range by controlling the size or functionalization at edge site [9], which make it accessible to be a narrow band-gap semiconductor for photocatalytic application [10,11]. Recently, the combination of GQDs with TiO2 is also reported to showing enhanced photocatalytic performance in the field of water splitting [12,13]and the degradation of pollutants [14]. In the binary GQDs-TiO2 heterojunction photocatalyst, it is generally concluded that GQDs not only play the role of photosensitizers to maximize the light harvest, but also act as an electron acceptor to receive photogenerated electrons from TiO2, thus achieve the efficient charge separation. However, despite the substantial progress, the photocatalytic activity over GQDs-TiO2 binary photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation is not improved remarkably because of the limited visible-light absorption ability of GQD. It is highly expected that the introduction of a new narrow band-gap semiconductor with strong visible-light absorption would improve the photocatalytic activity of the resulted ternary composite.

Cadmium sulfide (CdS), with a bandgap energy of 2.4 eV, has become one of the most efficient narrow band-gap semiconductors to couple with TiO2 for visible-light driven photocatalytic applications due to its suitable band position and superior visible-light absorption capability [15]. However, after a literature survey, we found that the combination of CdS, GQDs, and TiO2 to form ternary composite for photocatalytic application is insufficiently reported. Subramanian et al. [16] introduced GQDs as an interfacial layer between the TiO2 nanorods and CdS nanocrystals to improve the charge transport properties and greatly increase the photoelectrochemical responses of the resulted nanocomposites. Meng's group [17] using anodized TiO2 nanotubes-array as host to loading CdS and GQDs as monolithic photoanode for photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution. In the composite photocatalysts, the TiO2 not only play the role of UV absorber but also act as host, and their large surface area is very important for loading other active components. Hydrothermal synthesized titanate nanotubes (TNTs), as a kind of hydrous titanium oxide nanotubes, have been extensively explored as photocatalyst host to loading narrow band-gap semiconductors for photocatalyst application due to their large surface area, ion exchangeability, fast and long-distance electron transport [19–21]. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report about using titanate nanotubes as host to loading both CdS and GQDs for photocatalytic application.

Herein, we construct a ternary CdS-GQDs-TNTs nanocomposite for photocatlytic H2 production. It is well known that, for a hybrid photocatalyst, the unfavorable interfacial interaction by simply physical mixing has little contribution to the efficient charge carriers transfer. So, in order to achieve the intimate interaction between semiconductors by forming more effective chemical bonding, the successively in-situ growth of GQDs and CdS on TNTs are adopted, which is illustrated in Scheme 1 . In the first step, GQDs were in-situ formed on TNTs by solvothermal treatment of TNTs in DMF containing citric acid as carbon source. In the second step, CdS nanoparticles were in-situ deposited on TNTs by an ion-exchange reaction followed by sulfurization process, which is detailed in our previous work [18]. The obtained CdS-GQDs-TNTs nanocomposite was utilized as photocatalyst for H2 production under both simulated sunlight and visible light irradiation, and exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance compared to pure TNTs and binary GQDs-TNTs composite. The electron transfer behavior was clearly revealed to identify the governing factor for photo absorption, controlling charge transfer and separation, as well as photocatalytic activity in this CdS-GQDs-TNTs ternary photocatalyst.

All reagents are of analytical grade and used without further purification. Titanium oxide powder (anatase) was brought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Absolute ethanol (C2H5OH) was from Cologne Chemicals Co. LT. Thiourea (H2NCSNH2), citric acid monohydrate (CA: C6H8O7·H2O), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF: HCON(CH3)2) and ammonia solution (NH3) were obtained from Xilong Scientific. Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·H2O) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Deionized (DI) water was produced using Direct-Q3 water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs photocatalyst

ce:section.ce:para: Titanate nanotube (TNTs) was prepared by a typical hydrothermal method using commercial TiO2 as raw material. 2 g of TiO2 powder and 80 mL of 10 M NaOH solution were mixed and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, the sealed vessel was maintained at 150 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the product was washed with deionized water until pH ≈ 7. After that, the product was bathed in 0.1 M HCl solution for 5 h, and subsequently washed again until pH ≈ 7. The final product was obtained after drying at 80 °C.

The in-situ growth of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on TNTs was achieved by solvothermal treatment of as-prepared TNTs in DMF containing CA as carbon source, according to the literatures [22,23]. 0.35 g of CA were dissolved in 50 mL DMF and stirred to form a clear solution. Then, 1 g of TNTs was mixed with the solution by vigorous stirring for 1 h, and then transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed and maintained at 150 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the products were separated by centrifugation with absolute ethanol for several times at 9900 rpm. The final product was obtained by drying at 80 °C.

The in-situ deposited of CdS was achieved by an ion-exchange reaction followed by sulfurization process, according to our previous work [18]. A certain amount of ammonia solution was dropwise added into a 0.2 M CdCl2 aqueous solution to form a blue clear solution. Then, 0.4 g of GQDs-TNTs was dispersed into the clear solution by stirring at room temperature for 24 h. After washing with deionized water for several times, 0.2 M thiourea solution were added and ultrasonically treated for 1 h under vacuum conditions. Finally, the light yellow products (designated as CdS-GQDs-TNTs) were obtained after washing and drying at 80 °C.

The morphology of the samples was examined by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss HD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The composition and surface properties of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Mini Flex600 diffractometer), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with Al Kα X-ray radiation source, and Raman spectra (inVia, Renishaw) with a semiconductor laser excitation at 514 nm. N2 adsorption/desorption analysis was performed on a specific surface & pore size analyzer (3H–2000PS4, Beishide) analyzer at −196 °C using nitrogen. UV–Vis DRS spectra were recorded by spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu) equipped with an integration sphere. Photoluminescence spectra were collected by a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu) at room temperature.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production was carried out in an outer irradiation Pyrex glass reactor at room temperature. A 300 W Xenon lamp was used as the light source. In a typical experiment, 0.1 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in an 80 ml aqueous solution containing 0.1 M Na2S/Na2SO3 by ultrasound for 10 min. Before irradiation, the suspension was bubbled with N2 for 30 min to evacuate oxygen. The solution was continuously stirred during the whole reaction. The evolved gases were extracted every 15 min and analyzed by gas chromatography (Agilent 7890B) by thermal conductivity detector equipped with 5A molecular sieve using N2 as the carrier gas.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI750E, Shanghai, China) with a three-electrode cell made of quartz. A Pt sheet and a saturated Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The as-prepared samples were coated on FTO glasses by a doctor-blade method to serve as working electrodes with an active area of 1 cm2. 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was employed as the supporting electrolyte, which was bubbled with N2 for 30 min before tests. The transient photocurrent response was recorded at a bias of 0 V under chopped light irradiation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at a bias of −0.2 V with AC amplitude of 5 mV and a frequency range of 0.01–106 Hz. The Mott-Schottky analysis was recorded from −1.2 to 0.5 V at a frequency of 1 kHz in a solution containing 0.5 M Na2SO4.

The crystalline structures of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs were analyzed by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). For pure TNTs sample, all diffraction peaks exhibit a broad and relatively low intensity, which can be assigned to layered protonated titanate (JCPDS Card No. 47–0124) [24]. According to the report [25], the GQDs exhibit a strong diffraction peak at 2θ value of 26.3°. The GQDs-TNTs sample exhibit a similar XRD pattern to TNTs and no diffraction peaks of GQDs are present, which is possibly due to the small size of GQDs. Apart from the diffraction peaks of TNTs, CdS-GQDs-TNTs show three additional peaks centered at 26.5°, 43.9° and 52.0°, which marches well with the three major peaks of cubic CdS (JCPDS card No.65–2887). In order to identify the existence of GQDs, Raman spectra of GQDs-TNTs is recorded, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The Raman shift peaks at 394 cm−1 (B1g), 517 cm−1 (A1g), and 638 cm−1 (Eg) are ascribed to the characteristic peaks of titanate nanotubes. Two weak peaks centered at 1418 and 1650 cm−1 are ascribed to the D-band and G-band. According to the double resonance theory of graphene, the 1387 cm−1 in higher frequency of D-band is originates from disorders on the basal planes, and 1618 cm−1 in G-band is attributed to the LO phonon near K/4, which are typical features of graphene [26]. The blue shifts of the D-band and G-band (ca. 30 cm−1) in our experiments is probably due to the combined actions of quantum confinement effect and strongly interaction between TNTs and GQDs.

To further verify the morphology and microstructure of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs samples, TEM images have been taken. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the hydrothermal synthesized TNTs are tubular structure with a length ranging from several tens to hundreds of nanometers. The large-magnification image (see Fig. 2(b)) displays that TNTs are multi-walled nanotubes with an inter-shell spacing of around 0.73 nm. The outer diameter of the TNTs is measured to be about 10 nm, and the inner diameter of TNTs is approximately 4 nm. After solvothermal treatment of TNTs in DMF using CA as carbon source, the tubular morphology of TNTs is still maintained (see Fig. 2(c)), which is favorable to long-distance electron transport and mass transfer. What is more, GQDs (black dots in Fig. 2(c)) formed by intermolecular fusion of CA were found to decorated on surface of TNTs. The existence of GQDs in GQDs-TNTs sample is further confirmed by the HRTEM image, as seen in Fig. 2(d), where a 0.21 nm lattice spacing of graphene (100) planes is identified. The lateral size of GQDs is about 2.5 nm. For CdS-GQDs-TNTs sample, CdS quantum dots are only observed from TNTs outer surface (see Fig. 2(e)), because of their larger particle size than GQDs. The HRTEM image of CdS-GQDs-TNTs (Fig. 2(f)) shows that the CdS quantum dots is at the range of 5–10 nm. The lattice fringes of 0.336 nm belonging to CdS (111) planes and lattice fringes of 0.21 nm belonging to GQDs (1100) planes are clearly observed, suggesting the coexistence of CdS and GQDs on TNTs in ternary composite sample. The EDX further confirmed the existence of Ti, O, C, Cd and S elements in the CdS-GQDs-TNTs sample, as shown in Fig. S1.

The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs samples were analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption measurement. The isotherms of all samples exhibit a typical BDDT Type IV isotherm, according to IUPAC classification, with a type-H1 hysteresis hoop (see Fig. 3 (a)). The BET surface area of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs is 295, 206 and 159 m2 g−1, respectively. The successively decrease of SBET, suggesting that GQDs and CdS quantum dots are firmly decorated on the surface of TNTs. The pore-size distribution plots (Fig. 3(b)) were derived from the adsorption branch of the isotherm based on BJH method. The sharp peak at the range of 3–5 nm is ascribed to the inner cavity of the nanotubes, which is corresponding to the TEM results for the inner diameter of TNTs. The broad peak at the range of 6–20 nm could be attributed to pores between TNTs, which can be clearly observed from SEM image (see Fig. S2). The pore-size distribution of GQDs-TNTs is similar to that of TNTs, except that the pore volume is being slightly decreased at the pore diameter lower than 4 nm. This is probably due to the confinement of a small portion of GQDs inside the inner cavity of TNTs. For CdS-GQDs-TNTs, the pore volume dramatically decreased at the range of 5–20 nm, but increased at the range of 3–5 nm, which is probably due to the filling of CdS quantum dots into the pores between TNTs.

To determine the chemical compositions and chemical interactions among TNTs, GQDs and CdS quantum dots, XPS analysis of CdS-GQDs-TNTs were employed. The survey XPS spectrum shows strong signals of Ti 2p, O 1s and Cd 3d, as well as a weak signal of C 1s (see Fig. S3(a)). Fig. 4 (a–d) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, O 1s, C 1s, Cd 3d. As seen in Fig. 4(a), the photoelectron peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 centered at 458.7 and 464.4 eV, respectively, are typical for the Ti4+–O bonds in TNTs lattice [27]. In the O 1s spectra (see Fig. 4(b)), the peaks at 530.3 and 532.0 eV can be ascribe to Ti–O–Ti bonds in the TNTs lattice and Ti–O–C bonds on its surface, respectively, according to the literatures [28–30]. Fig. 4(c) shows the C1s spectrum of CdS-GQDs-TNTs, which is fitted with four Gaussian peaks centered at 284.8, 286.2, 288.6, and 289.2 eV. The peak at 284.8 eV is assigned to sp2 bonded carbon (CC), whereas the other peaks at 286.2, 288.6, 289.2 eV are attributed to the oxygen related edge functional groups in GQDs, such as C–O (ether), CO, and COOH, respectively [29,31–34]. No Ti–C bond related peak (282 eV) is observed, implying that GQDs were anchored to the surface via Ti–O–C bonds instead of enter the lattice of TNTs [14]. In the Cd 3d spectra (see Fig. 4(d)), the peaks located at 405.2 and 411.9 eV for Cd 3d5/2 and for Cd 3d3/2, respectively, can be assigned to Cd–S bonds arising from CdS quantum dots [35]. These two peaks are slightly higher than pure CdS (see Fig. S3(b)), for which the Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 appear at 405.1 and 411.8 eV, respectively. The shift of Cd 3d binding energy in the composite of CdS-GQDs-TNTs relative to pure CdS indicated the electronic effect/interaction between CdS quantum dots and TNTs [27].

The optical adsorption properties of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs samples were analyzed by UV–Vis DRS spectra (see Fig. 5 (a)). The pure TNTs exhibit only UV response with an onset at a wavelength of 380 nm. Both GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs demonstrate a broad visible-light absorption ranging from 380 to 550 nm, which is related to the intrinsic band-gap photoexcitation of GQDs and CdS quantum dots. Compared to GQDs-TNTs, the absorption intensity of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is dramatically increased, suggesting that CdS is dominated in visible-light harvest in the ternary composite. The band-gap of the samples was estimated by the Kubelka–Munk function [36]:
(1)
α h v =
C
1
(
h v −
E
g
)
2
where α,
h
,
v
,
E
g
and C 1 are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band-gap energy, and a constant, respectively. Fig. 5 (b) shows
(
α h v
)
1
/
2
plot versus
h v
and the tangent segment is extended to X-axis to obtain the indirect band-gap value, which is 3.40, 2.82, and 2.36 eV for TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, and CdS-GQDs-TNTs, respectively.

The intimate interfacial contact between semiconductors is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, which can be determined by photoelectrochemical measurements. PL spectrum could reflect some combination of semiconductor photoelectron and hole. The higher intensity corresponds to the faster recombination [27]. Fig. 6 (a) shows the PL spectra of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The emission peaks of GQDs-TNTs are similar to that of TNTs at the range of 380–650 nm, but the PL intensity is slightly decreased for GQDs-TNTs. When CdS quantum dots are incorporated, the PL intensity of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is dramatically decreased, which suggest the longer lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs. Fig. 6(b) shows the transient photocurrent response of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, CdS-GQDs-TNTs samples under simulated sunlight irradiation. After loading GQDs to TNTs, the photocurrent density is enhanced, which is 1.5 time higher than that of pure TNTs, which is ascribed to the extended visible light harvesting and enhanced interfacial charge separation at the GQDs/TNTs heterojunction. The photocurrent density of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is further increased, which is 1.7 time higher than that of GQDs-TNTs. The enhanced photocurrent response is attributed to the greatly enhanced visible light absorption and enhanced interfacial charge separation at the CdS/TNTs heterojunction. However, the photocurrent density of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is decreased with irradiation time as a result of photocorrosion of CdS in Na2SO4 solution. The photocurrent responses of the three samples exhibit a similar trend under visible-light irradiation, except that the photocurrent density is lower for one order of magnitude compared to the results under simulated sunlight irradiation. It is noticeable that, the photocurrent density of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is greatly enhanced under visible-light illumination, which is 5 time higher than that of GQDs-TNTs. The result further confirmed that CdS quantum dots is dominated in visible-light harvest in the ternary composite, which is corresponding to the UV–Vis adsorption results. The separation of photogenerated charges through CdS-TNTs interface is more efficient than GQDs-TNTs interface, which is proved by EIS results. Fig. 6(d) shows that the Nyquist plots of all samples exhibit a single semicircle in the low-frequency region. And the CdS-GQDs-TNTs ternary exhibit the smallest circular arc, which represents a smallest carrier transfer resistance at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface [37,38].

The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities of the samples were evaluated under both simulated sunlight and visible-light irradiation using 0.1 M Na2S/Na2SO3 as sacrificial reagents. Fig. 7 (a) shows the time courses of H2 evolution over TNTs, GQDs-TNTs, CdS-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNT sunder simulated sunlight irradiation for 8 h. For both pure TNTs and GQDs-TNTs, the hydrogen evolution rates are linearly increased in the first 2 h and gradually decreased in the following 6 h. The total amount of H2 evolution during the 8 h reaction is approximately 1563 and 2321 μmol/g for TNTs and GQDs-TNTs, respectively. The enhanced hydrogen production activity for GQDs-TNTs is ascribed to the extended visible-light harvest and fast separation photo-generated electrons and holes at the GQDs/TNTs heterojunction. When CdS quantum dots and GQDs are both decorated on TNTs, the H2 evolution activity and stability are obvious enhanced, which shows a linear increase during 8 h reaction with a total amount of H2 evolution of 4241 μmol/g. The hydrogen production yield of CdS-GQDs-TNTs composite is higher than that of CdS-TNTs, which indicted the positive role of GQDs in CdS-GQDs-TNTs composite. The stability of CdS-GQDs-TNTs photocatalyst was examined by cycling runs for the photocatalytic H2 evolution. The results showed that the photocatalytic performance of CdS-GQDs-TNTs photocatalyst can be very stable after four cycles (see Fig. S4).

Fig. 7(b) compares the H2 evolution over GQDs-TNTs and CdS-GQDs-TNTs under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). The GQDs-TNTs exhibits a continuously hydrogen production for 5 h, but ceased after that, which is probably ascribed to the exhausting of sacrificial agent. Compared to GQDs-TNTs, CdS-GQDs-TNTs displays a higher photocatalytic activity and stability under both visible-light and simulated sunlight irradiation, which are ascribed to the longer lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs, the stronger visible-light harvest of CdS, and more efficient charges separation at CdS/TNTs interface than at GQDs/TNTs.

The enhanced photocatalytic performance of CdS-GQDs-TNTs is highly dependent on the photon absorption, efficient interfacial charge transfer and transport. In order to reveal the essential charges transfer mechanism, Mott-Schottky measurements were performed to determine the energy position of semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 8 , the M − S curves of pure TNTs, pure CdS and GQDs-TNTs electrodes exhibit a positive slope, implying a typical n-type semiconductor behavior. The flat-band potentials of the TNTs and GQDs-TNTs electrodes were calculated using the Mott–Schottky relationship [41]:
(2)
1
/
C
2
=
2
(
E −
E
f b
−
k T
/
e
)
/
e
ε
r
ε
0
N
D
where C is the capacitance,
ε
r
is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor,
ε
0
is the vacuum permittivity, N
D is the donor density, k is the Boltzmann constant, T is room temperature,
e
is electronic charge, E is the applied potential, and
E
f b
is the flat-band potential. Based on the M − S plots, the
E
f b
values were obtained by extrapolating the linear part of the curves to X-axis [36,42], which is determined to be −0.49 V, −0.73 V and −0.61 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for pure TNTs, GQDs and pure CdS, respectively. It is reported that the bottom of the conduction bands (CB) can be estimated to be about 0.1 V less than the
E
f b
for many n-type semiconductors [39,40]. Based on this assumption, the CB energy level of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and pure CdS are calculated to be −0.39 eV, −0.63 eV and −0.51 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Therefore, the valence band (VB) levels of TNTs, GQDs-TNTs and pure CdS are calculated to be 3.01 eV, 2.19 eV and 1.85 eV (vs. NHE), according to the equation
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E
g
, where
E
g
is the band-gap energy obtained by Kubelka–Munk function spectra (see Fig. 5(b)). Based on the above results, the energy band configuration of TNTs, GQDs and CdS semiconductors are quantitatively drawn and the charges separation and transfer process in ternary CdS-GQDs-TNTs photocatalyst are illustrated in Scheme 2 . Due to the quantum confinement effect, GQDs with a size of 2.5 nm can be used as a narrow-band semiconductor [42]. The 1D TNTs play the role of host, and the 0D CdS quantum dots and GQDs both act as photosensitizers. Under visible light irradiation, both CdS and GQDs harvest visible-light to generate e−/h+ pairs. Then the efficient photogenerated charge carriers separation are achieved through the heterojunction of CdS/TNTs and GQDs/TNTs. As shown in Scheme 2, the CB levels of GQDs and CdS are both more negative than that of TNTs, giving a large energy difference to drive the electron injections from the CB of GQDs and CdS to that of TNTs. In this sense, the GQDs play the role of electron donor instead of acceptor reported by other groups [7,43]. Meanwhile, since the VB levels of TNTs are higher than that of both CdS and GQDs, the photogenerated holes would transfer at the opposite direction, which is from VB of TNTs to that of GQDs and CdS, respectively. The photoelectrons transport to the surface active site of TNTs is facilitated along the 1D nanotubes. Finally, the electrons on the surface of TNTs reduce H+ to produce H2, simultaneously, the holes in the surface of CdS and GQDs are consumed by sacrificial agent (SO3 2− and S2−) [16]. The combined interaction of the extended visible light harvesting, prolonged lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs, efficient interfacial charge transfer, fast and long-distance electron transport pass contribute to the enhanced H2 evolution activity of the ternary CdS-GQDs-TNTs heterojunction photocatalyst.

In summary, the strongly coupled ternary GQDs-TNTs-CdS photocatalyst was successfully prepared through the in situ growth strategy. The GQDs are firmly decorated on both inner and outer surface of TNT through the formation of Ti–O–C chemical bonding, and CdS QDs are loaded on the outer surface of TNTs through strong interfacial interaction. The photoelectrochemical measurements proved that GQDs and CdS QDs are both play the role of photosensitizer to extend the visible light response, and CdS QDs dominated in visible-light harvest. The intimate interaction further contributed the formation of CdS/TNTs and GQDs/TNTs heterojunctions, which facilitate the fast interfacial electrons and holes separation. And the charges separation at CdS/TNTs interface is more efficient than at GQDs/TNTs interface. The ternary hybrid CdS-GQDs-TNTs exhibits enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity and stability under both visible-light and simulated sunlight irradiation, which is ascribed to the combined interaction of the extended visible-light absorption ability, the prolonged lifetime of photogenerated electron−hole pairs, fast interfacial charge transfer rate, and 1D electron transport pass. The charges transfer mechanism suggested that CdS QDs and GQDs are both play the role of donor to supply photoinduced electrons for TNTs. This work not only provides an innovative strategy to synthesize strongly coupled ternary hybrid composite, but also offers a new insight to understand the essential roles of CdS and GQDs in GQDs-based nanohybrids. The study on the charges transfer mechanism would provide useful guidance for the design more efficient ternary materials toward energy and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (Grant No. 2018GXNSFBA138007, 2018GXNSFBA281073, 2018GXNSFAA294021), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.11665007, 11664003), the State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University, CMEMR2018-B11), and the PhD Start-up Fund of Guangxi Normal University (Grant No. 2017BQ023, 2017BQ006), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. XYCSZ2019058).
